content.full_text:
FPPC

CITY OF OCEANSIDE

VERA M.I. TODOROV
DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY

CHARLES R. REVLETT
CITY ATTORNEY

JOSEPH J. STINE
DEPUTY CITY ATTORNEY

DEBRA E. CORBETT
ASSISTANT CITY ATTORNEY

ELIAS J. SANCHEZ
LEGAL ASSISTANT

MICHAEL W. BARRETT
LAW CLERK

June 25, 1990

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request for Informal Advice

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

This letter is written to request informal advice from the
Fair Political Practices Commission pursuant to Government Code
section 83114(b) and 2 Cal. Code of Regs. 18329 on behalf of the
City of Oceanside.

The question posed to the Commission is as follows: Is an
independent contractor, Company A, considered a City employee for
the purposes of the disqualification requirements of the
Political Reform Act where Company A is a consulting engineer of
the City, is designated as the project engineer for Project X,
has designed Project X and has prepared the plans and
specifications for Project X? And, if so, is Company A
disqualified from participating in any decision-making processes
related to Project X because it has a prohibited financial
interest in Project X?

The facts are as follows: The principles of Company A have
an ownership interest in Company B, which may be the low bidder
at a future bid opening for the construction of Project X. More
specifically, two of the principles in Company A each have a 33-
1/3 percent ownership interest in Company B. As project engineer
on behalf of the City for Project X, Company A is responsible for
handling all matters relating to design questions, change orders,
price increases, construction materials, adequacy of the work,
and have ultimate responsibility for the job, including but not
limited to the construction phase of the project.

No authorization for this request for informal advice is
attached because we find that this situation may potentially
occur with several independent contractors of the City of
Oceanside. The City is especially interested in preserving the
integrity of the competitive bidding process for its projects.

300 NORTH HILL STREET • OCEANSIDE, CA 92054 • FAX 619-966-4436 • PHONE 619-966-4444

Scott Hallabrin
June 25, 1990
Page two

     Please do not hesitate to contact the undersigned if you
have any questions about this request.  Thank you in advance for
your prompt attention to this matter.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          CHARLES R. REVLETT
                                          City Attorney
                                          [signature]
                                          VERA M. I. TODOROV
                                          Deputy City Attorney

cc:  City Attorney
     City Manager
     Water and Sewer Director
     City Engineer
     Law Clerk

sections.question: Is an independent contractor, Company A, considered a City employee for the purposes of the disqualification requirements of the Political Reform Act where Company A is a consulting engineer of the City, is designated as the project engineer for Project X, has designed Project X and has prepared the plans and specifications for Project X? And, if so, is Company A disqualified from participating in any decision-making processes related to Project X because it has a prohibited financial interest in Project X?
sections.question_synthetic: Whether an independent contractor serving as a consulting engineer and project engineer for a city project, whose principals have ownership interests in a company bidding on that project's construction, is considered a city employee and whether such contractor is disqualified from participating in project-related decisions due to prohibited financial interests.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request from a city attorney to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. No conclusion/advice section is present. Document is dated June 25, 1990 and references Government Code section 83114(b).

embedding.summary: The City of Oceanside's Deputy City Attorney requests informal FPPC advice regarding whether an independent contractor consultant (Company A) serving as project engineer is a city employee subject to disqualification rules, given that principals in Company A have ownership interests in a company that may bid on the project's construction.
